title: Agricultural products – revision of EU marketing standards (Breakfast Directives)
bodyHtml:
According to the letter received from the Ministry of Trade, the European Commission has proposed a directive amendment on honey, fruit juices, jams, jellies, marmalades, sweetened chestnut puree, and partially or wholly dehydrated milks, referred to as "Breakfast Directives," related to their composition, sales names, labeling, and market presentation rules. The proposal aims to adapt these rules to changing consumer concerns, such as healthier nutrition and sustainable food options. It is mentioned that the proposal will require the suppliers of these products, including those from Turkey, in the EU market to review their current labeling practices and also consider the fruit content in final products, especially in jams.

Furthermore, the letter states that the key highlights of the new rules aimed at promoting healthier nutrition among EU citizens under the Farm to Fork Strategy are as follows:

For honey, in cases where a product is a blend of honeys from multiple countries, the product can be labeled as "Blend of EU honeys," "Blend of non-EU honeys," or "Blend of EU and non-EU honeys," with the requirement to indicate the origin countries on the label for all package sizes except those below 25 grams.

For fruit juices and nectars, despite the confusing differentiation between fruit nectars allowing added sugars and fruit juices not containing added sugars, the Commission allows the statement "fruit juices do not contain added sugars" to be used on fruit juice products. It also mandates the labeling of fruit juice and fruit juice blends derived from concentrate(s) as "from concentrate(s)" or "partially from concentrate(s)," and the creation of a new category for fruit juices from which naturally occurring sugars have been removed. The allowed amount of added sugar and/or honey is proposed to be reduced from 20% to 15% of the total weight for low-acid, high-fiber, or highly flavored fruits and naturally unpalatable fruit juices. Additionally, sunflower seed is proposed to be added to the list of permitted processing aids and substances for fruit juices, and the use of the terms "coconut water/juice" in compliance with the relevant Codex Standard is requested.

For fruit jams, jellies, and marmalades, the Commission proposes increasing the minimum fruit content for jams and extra jams, allowing the soluble dry matter content of jams/jellies to be less than 60% under certain circumstances, and permitting member states to use the terms "jam" and "marmalade" interchangeably. The addition of concentrated citrus fruit juice to the list of additional ingredients allowed in jam/marmalade production is also requested.

For prepared milks, the Commission proposes allowing the reduction of lactose content by converting it into glucose and galactose, but with the possibility of restrictions or bans by certain EU member states. Additionally, it is requested that the term "evaporated milk" in English be considered synonymous with "condensed milk."

For more information on the Breakfast Directives Proposal and feedback, please click the provided link.
